topic: Properties and Functions of the Cell (Continued)

subUnits.title: 7.1 Micro and Macro Nutrients and Respiration
notes: Nutrients are substances required by organisms for growth, maintenance, and reproduction. They are classified based on the quantity required.
• Definition: Nutrients required in large quantities by organisms
- Nitrogen (N): Component of proteins, chlorophyll, nucleic acids
- Phosphorus (P): Component of ATP, nucleic acids, cell membranes
- Potassium (K): Enzyme activation, water balance, stomatal function
- Calcium (Ca): Cell wall structure, membrane function
- Magnesium (Mg): Component of chlorophyll, enzyme activation
- Sulfur (S): Component of proteins and vitamins
- Fats: Energy storage, insulation, cell membranes
- Nitrogen deficiency: Yellowing leaves (chlorosis), stunted growth
- Phosphorus deficiency: Poor root development, purple leaves
- Potassium deficiency: Weak stems, leaf scorching
- Nigerian example: Yellowing of maize leaves due to nitrogen deficiency
- Fat deficiency: Poor absorption of fat-soluble vitamins
- Nigerian example: Protein-energy malnutrition in children
• Definition: Nutrients required in small quantities but essential for normal growth
- Zinc (Zn): Enzyme activation, growth hormone production
- Boron (B): Cell wall formation, fruit development
- Iron deficiency: Interveinal chlorosis in young leaves
- Zinc deficiency: Stunted growth, small leaves
- Nigerian example: Iron deficiency in crops grown in alkaline soils
- Vitamin A deficiency: Night blindness, poor immunity
- Nigerian example: Iron deficiency anemia common in Nigerian women
Respiration is the biochemical process by which living organisms break down food substances to release energy for life processes.
• Definition: Exchange of gases (O₂ and CO₂) between organism and environment
• In plants: Occurs through stomata and lenticels
• Nigerian example: Oxygen diffusion into fish gills from water in Nigerian rivers
• Definition: Breakdown of food molecules within cells to release energy
• Occurs in mitochondria of eukaryotic cells
• Two types: Aerobic and anaerobic respiration
• Definition: First stage of cellular respiration occurring in cytoplasm
- Glucose (6-carbon) is broken down into two pyruvate molecules (3-carbon each)
- Requires 2 ATP, produces 4 ATP (net gain: 2 ATP)
- Does not require oxygen (occurs in both aerobic and anaerobic respiration)
• Nigerian context: Glycolysis occurs in all living cells, including those of Nigerian crops and livestock
• Definition: Respiration that occurs in the presence of oxygen
- Series of reactions producing CO₂, ATP, NADH, FADH₂
- Oxygen is final electron acceptor, forming water
- Produces large amount of ATP (oxidative phosphorylation)
• Energy yield: ~36-38 ATP molecules per glucose molecule
• Nigerian example: Human muscle cells during normal activity; cassava root cells during growth
• Definition: Respiration that occurs in the absence of oxygen
- Occurs in muscle cells during intense exercise when oxygen is limited
- Pyruvate is converted to lactic acid
- Nigerian example: Athletes experiencing muscle cramps during sports
- Occurs in yeast, some bacteria, and plant roots in waterlogged soils
- Pyruvate is converted to ethanol and CO₂
- Nigerian example: Yeast fermentation in local brewing (burukutu, kunun-zaki); root respiration in flooded rice fields
• Energy yield: Only 2 ATP molecules per glucose molecule (much less efficient than aerobic)
Difference Between Aerobic and Anaerobic Respiration:
• End products: Lactic acid (animals) or ethanol + CO₂ (plants/yeast)
Role of Enzymes in Cellular Respiration:
• Definition: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions
- Krebs Cycle: Citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase
- Electron Transport Chain: ATP synthase, cytochrome oxidase
• Nigerian context: Enzyme deficiencies can cause metabolic disorders in humans and animals
• Teacher sets experiments to show respiration in yeast (anaerobic) and respiration in rat (aerobic)
• Mix yeast with warm water and sugar
• Test for CO₂ production using lime water
• Nigerian context: Relate to local brewing processes
• Teacher demonstrates to students action of ptyalin on cooked starch
• Students produce saliva from their mouths to carry out the experiment
• Observe breakdown of starch to simpler sugars
• Teacher draws the Krebs cycle on the chalkboard
• Students draw also and label the stages
• Discussion: What is the importance of each stage?
• 'Respiration is the same as breathing': Respiration is cellular; breathing is mechanical
• 'Plants only respire at night': Plants respire continuously; photosynthesis only occurs in light
• 'Anaerobic respiration is useless': It provides emergency energy when oxygen is limited
• 'Enzymes are consumed in reactions': Enzymes are catalysts and are not used up
• Understanding nutrient requirements helps improve crop yields in Nigerian agriculture
• Knowledge of respiration explains energy needs in humans, livestock, and crops
• Enzyme knowledge important for food processing and biotechnology in Nigeria
• Nutrient deficiency awareness important for public health education
• Micro and macro nutrients are essential for growth and metabolism
• Respiration is the process that releases energy from food for cellular activities
• Understanding these concepts has applications in agriculture, health, and industry
• In Nigeria, this knowledge supports food security, public health, and scientific advancement

quiz.q: Macro nutrients are required by organisms in:
quiz.explanation: Macro nutrients are required in large quantities for growth, energy, and maintenance of organisms.

quiz.q: A deficiency of nitrogen in plants causes:
quiz.explanation: Nitrogen deficiency causes chlorosis (yellowing) because nitrogen is a component of chlorophyll.

quiz.q: The first stage of cellular respiration is:
quiz.explanation: Glycolysis is the first stage of cellular respiration, occurring in the cytoplasm and breaking down glucose into pyruvate.

quiz.q: Aerobic respiration produces approximately how many ATP molecules?
quiz.explanation: Aerobic respiration yields about 36-38 ATP molecules per glucose molecule, making it much more efficient than anaerobic respiration.

quiz.q: Anaerobic respiration in human muscle cells produces:
quiz.explanation: During intense exercise when oxygen is limited, human muscle cells produce lactic acid through anaerobic respiration.

quiz.q: Enzymes in cellular respiration function to:
quiz.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up the chemical reactions of respiration without being consumed.

quiz.q: A Nigerian example of anaerobic respiration is:
quiz.explanation: Yeast ferments sugars anaerobically to produce ethanol and CO₂ in traditional Nigerian brewing processes.
Iron is essential for hemoglobin production; deficiency causes anemia, characterized by fatigue and weakness.

quiz.q: The Krebs cycle occurs in the:
quiz.explanation: The Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle) occurs in the mitochondrial matrix of eukaryotic cells.

quiz.q: Understanding nutrient requirements is important for Nigerian agriculture because it:
opts: Guides fertilizer application and crop improvement
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of nutrient requirements helps farmers optimize soil fertility and crop yields through appropriate nutrient management.
